title: Body Storming: 90 Seconds of Permission
dek: *What if the anger you've been managing so carefully doesn't need to be managed at all, but simply felt? Body storming is a counter-intuitive practice that asks you to stop controlling your rage for exactly 90 seconds and let your body do what it already knows how to do. In our experience, this brief window of somatic permission changes everything about how anger moves through you.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Body storming is a 90-second practice where you give yourself full permission to feel anger physically without needing to justify or explain it. The body carries emotional tension in predictable patterns, and brief somatic discharge prevents chronic stress accumulation. Regular practice rewires your nervous system's relationship to anger, transforming suppression into skillful expression.

What would happen if you let yourself feel the full force of your anger for just 90 seconds, without commentary, without apology, without needing it to make sense? Most of us have spent decades learning to do the opposite. We've been taught that anger is dangerous, that good people don't get furious, that losing control means losing ourselves. So we compress it. We rationalize it. We turn it into headaches and jaw tension and that chronic tightness between the shoulder blades that no amount of massage seems to touch. Body storming flips that script. It's not anger management. It's anger permission. The practice is deceptively simple: you set a timer for 90 seconds, you move into a private space, and you let your body express whatever it's holding without filtering, without performing, without needing to understand why. You might shake. You might make sounds that surprise you. You might discover your fists want to pound something soft, or your feet want to stomp, or your voice wants to roar in a way you haven't allowed since childhood. And then the timer goes off, and you're done. ## Why 90 Seconds Matters (And What Happens in Your Nervous System) Neuroscientist Jill Bolte Taylor observed that the physiological lifespan of an emotion, from trigger to chemical flush, is about 90 seconds. After that, any remaining emotional experience is because we're re-triggering the response by looping the thought that sparked it. Your body knows how to complete the anger cycle. It's your mind that keeps restarting it. When you give yourself 90 seconds of pure somatic permission, you're working with your nervous system's natural rhythm rather than against it. The sympathetic activation that comes with anger, the surge of adrenaline and cortisol, the increased heart rate and blood pressure, all of these are designed to mobilize you for action. When that action is chronically blocked, when you swallow the roar every single time, PubMed research on anger expression and cardiovascular load shows measurable impacts on your heart and vascular system. Pat Ogden, founder of Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, emphasizes that trauma and emotional overwhelm get stored in procedural memory, in the body's movement patterns and postural habits. Talk therapy can help you understand your anger, but it often can't help you discharge it. The body needs to move the anger through in the language the body speaks: sensation, gesture, sound, physical release. In my own practice, I've watched clients discover that their anger has a very specific physical signature. One person's rage lives in their throat as a scream that's been held back since adolescence. Another's sits in their pelvis as a deep, earthquake-like trembling. Someone else finds it in their hands, which want to tear or throw or build something new from the wreckage. Body storming gives you 90 seconds to speak that somatic language fluently. ## The Mechanics: How to Actually Do This You'll need: - A timer (your phone works fine) - A private space where you won't be interrupted - Optional: a pillow or cushion to hit, stomp on, or throw - Optional: a towel you can twist or tear Set your timer for 90 seconds. Not two minutes, not five. Exactly 90 seconds. Close the door. Turn off notifications. This is your time. Take one deep breath, and then drop into your body. Where do you feel the anger right now? In your jaw? Your chest? Your hands? Your belly? Start there. Let that part of your body lead. If your jaw is clenched, let it clench harder. Exaggerate it. Make a sound if one wants to come. If your fists are tight, let them pound. If your feet want to stomp, stomp like you're five years old and someone just told you no for the tenth time today. If you want to yell, yell. If you want to growl or grunt or make sounds that don't have words, do that. This is not performance. No one is watching. You're not trying to look a certain way or do it "right." You're just letting your body say what it's been holding. The timer will feel both endless and impossibly short. Let it run. When it goes off, stop. Shake out your hands. Take three slow breaths. Notice what's different. Notice what your body feels like now compared to 90 seconds ago. That's it. That's the whole practice. ## What Body Storming Is Not Let's be clear about boundaries, because this practice can sound reckless if you've spent your life afraid of your own anger. Body storming is not: - An excuse to hurt yourself or anyone else - A license to break things you value or destroy property - A way to bypass genuine anger work or therapy - A substitute for addressing the actual sources of your rage - A performance for others or a way to punish someone with your anger It's a private, boundaried, time-limited somatic practice. You're not acting out your anger on the world. You're giving your body 90 seconds to express what it's been carrying so that you can then engage with the world from a more regulated place. Gabor Maté writes extensively about how repressed anger becomes the body's disease, how the inability to say no, to set boundaries, to express healthy rage at violation, correlates with everything from autoimmune conditions to cancer. He's not suggesting we all become rage-fueled tyrants. He's pointing out that emotional suppression has a physiological cost, and that cost compounds over decades. Body storming is one way to stop accumulating that debt. ## The Sensation Map: What Anger Feels Like When You Stop Controlling It The first time you body storm, you might be surprised by what emerges. In our experience, most people discover their anger has texture, temperature, color, and very specific movement patterns they've never consciously noticed before. Some people feel heat rising from their belly into their chest, like a furnace door swinging open. Others feel cold, sharp, crystalline rage that wants to cut or shatter something. Some feel their anger as heavy, molten, volcanic. Others feel it as electric, buzzing, sparking in their fingertips and jaw. Pay attention to these qualities. They're your anger's signature. One client described her rage as red and spiky, living in her shoulders and wanting to push everything away. Another said his felt like black tar in his throat, thick and suffocating, desperate to be screamed out. Someone else found hers in her legs, a trembling that wanted to run or kick or stomp the earth until it listened. Your anger is not generic. It's as unique as your fingerprint, and body storming teaches you to read it. When you know what your anger feels like in your body, you can catch it earlier. You can notice the first flicker of heat or the first tightening in your jaw and think, "Ah, there you are. I see you." And then you can choose: Do I need to body storm right now, or can I note this and tend to it later? Do I need to set a boundary with the person in front of me, or is this old anger from something else entirely? The practice gives you fluency in your own nervous system. ## Pairing Body Storming with Somatic Support Some people find that [doTERRA Frankincense Essential Oil (15 mL)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B003I6Q3IG?tag=spankyspinola-20) helps ground them before or after a body storming session, the warm, resinous scent creating a sensory anchor for the nervous system. Others use [Bach Original Flower Remedies, Oak - Restore Endurance - Natural Homeopathic Flo](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005P0UPGY?tag=spankyspinola-20) when they notice they've been white-knuckling their way through life, pushing through exhaustion and resentment without pause. Oak essence supports people who've forgotten how to rest, who've turned endurance into a religion, and who need permission to stop being so relentlessly strong. Over the years I've seen clients pair body storming with [Solgar 5-HTP 100 mg (alt SKU)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000Z94138?tag=spankyspinola-20), especially when their anger is entangled with depression or when the somatic release leaves them feeling emotionally raw. The serotonin support can smooth the edges without numbing the experience. You can explore more about [our approach to herbal support](/herbs) and how different plants companion different emotional states. There's no one-size-fits-all formula. Your body will tell you what it needs if you're willing to listen. ## The Difference Between Venting and Discharging This distinction matters, so let's slow down here. Venting is when you tell the story of your anger over and over. You rehash what happened, you explain why you're furious, you loop the narrative until your friends are exhausted and you're more wound up than when you started. Venting keeps you in your head. It re-triggers the anger without completing it. Body storming is discharge. It's somatic, not narrative. You're not explaining your anger to anyone, including yourself. You're letting your body move the activation through and out. Harriet Lerner, in her work on the dance of anger, emphasizes that anger is often a signal pointing to something important, a boundary violation or a pattern that needs to change. She's right. Anger carries information. But before you can hear that information clearly, you often need to discharge the physiological overwhelm so you're not trying to think through a nervous system on fire. Body storm first. Then, once your system has settled, you can ask: What is this anger trying to tell me? What boundary do I need to set? What pattern needs to shift? The practice doesn't replace the cognitive work. It makes the cognitive work possible. ## When Body Storming Becomes a Daily Ritual Some people body storm once and feel a profound shift. Others discover it needs to become a regular practice, like brushing your teeth or stretching in the morning. If you've spent decades suppressing anger, you're probably carrying more than one 90-second session can touch. That's normal. That's not failure. That's just an honest acknowledgment of how much you've been holding. Try this: body storm every day for a week. Same time, same private space. Set the timer, drop in, let your body lead. Don't judge what comes up. Don't compare Tuesday's rage to Thursday's. Just show up and let it move. After a week, notice what's different. Are you sleeping better? Is your jaw less tight? Are you quicker to notice when you're angry instead of discovering it three days later as a tension headache? Across the articles we've published on this site, we return again and again to the idea that emotional health is not about feeling good all the time. It's about feeling what's actually true in your body and having practices that help you metabolize those feelings instead of storing them as chronic tension. Body storming is one of those practices. If you're curious about other somatic and emotional assessments that can help you map your unique nervous system patterns, [check out the assessments hub](/assessments) where we've gathered tools for everything from attachment style to stress response. ## The 90-Second Permission Slip You've Been Waiting For Here's what nobody tells you about anger: it doesn't want to destroy you. It wants to move through you. Your body already knows how to do this. You've just been taught to override that knowing for so long that it feels dangerous to trust it again. Body storming is permission. Permission to be bigger than you've allowed yourself to be. Permission to take up space. Permission to let your body speak in its first language, the one that predates words and manners and all the ways you've learned to make yourself small and acceptable. Ninety seconds. That's all. You can be furious for 90 seconds and still be a good person. You can let your body roar and still be kind. You can practice somatic discharge and still be thoughtful about how you engage with conflict in your relationships. The practice doesn't make you more angry. It makes you more honest about the anger that's already there. ## Frequently Asked Questions About Body Storming ### What if I start body storming and can't stop after 90 seconds? Honor the timer anyway. Part of the practice is learning that you can feel intense emotion and still regulate yourself, that you can touch the fire without being consumed by it. If you're genuinely concerned about your ability to stop, start with 30-second sessions and work your way up. If stopping feels impossible, that's information worth taking to a trauma-informed therapist who can help you build more capacity for titrated emotional release. ### Can I body storm around other people or does it have to be private? In our experience, body storming works best when you're alone and unobserved. The practice requires a level of unselfconsciousness that's almost impossible to access when you're performing for an audience, even an audience of one trusted person. Privacy gives you permission to be ugly, to be incoherent, to make sounds and movements that don't make sense. That said, some people do body storm with a therapist present, especially when they're first learning the practice and need that containment. ### What if I body storm and don't feel anything or can't access my anger? That's common, especially if you've been disconnected from your anger for a long time. Don't force it. Instead, try placing your hands on the parts of your body where you think you might be holding tension (jaw, shoulders, belly, throat) and just notice. Sometimes anger needs an invitation rather than a demand. You might also explore whether what you're calling "nothing" is actually numbness or frozenness, which are their own kind of protective response worth acknowledging. ## What Happens After the Storm You'll know body storming is working not because you stop getting angry, but because your relationship to anger shifts. You'll catch yourself noticing the first stirrings of rage instead of discovering it three days later as a migraine. You'll set a boundary in real time instead of swallowing your no and resenting the person for weeks. You'll feel the heat rise in your chest and think, "I need to body storm later," and then actually do it instead of letting it accumulate into chronic background fury. The practice doesn't make you calm. It makes you current. Your anger stops being this dangerous, shameful thing you have to manage and becomes information, energy, a force that can be channeled and expressed and released. It becomes something you can work with instead of something you're constantly working against. And here's the surprising part: when you stop being afraid of your own anger, you often discover you're less angry overall. Not because you're suppressing it better, but because you're not carrying decades of backlogged rage everywhere you go. You're current. You're discharging in real time. You're letting the storm pass through instead of trying to contain it until you explode. If you want to explore more practices that honor the body's wisdom and work with emotional intensity rather than against it, [visit us at theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) where we dive deeper into somatic spirituality and the places where rage and grace meet. You can also [learn more about our approach](/about) and the philosophy that shapes everything we offer here. Ninety seconds. A timer, a closed door, and the willingness to let your body tell the truth it's been holding. That's all you need. The permission slip you've been waiting for? You're holding it right now. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on body storming: 90 seconds of permission, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
